MORRISON & FOERSTER
ATTORNEYS AT LAW

SAN FRANCISCO
ORANGE COUNTY
WALNUT CREEK
PALO ALTO
DENVER

333 SOUTH GRAND AVENUE
LOS ANGELES, CA 90071-3168
TELEPHONE (213) 626-3800
TELEFACSIMILE (213) 626-6955
TELEX 261823 MOFO UR

NEW YORK
WASHINGTON, D.C.
LONDON
HONG KONG
TOKYO

DIRECT DIAL NUMBER

August 2, 1991

(213) 621-9577

VIA FEDERAL EXPRESS

Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, California 95804-0807
Attention: John Wallace, Esq.

Re: Letter No. 91-118

Dear Mr. Wallace:

This letter is in reference to Letter No. 91-118
(the "Request"), a request for informal assistance, dated
February 22, 1991, submitted by Robert K. Olsen of our firm
to Mark T. Morodomi, an attorney with the Commission. This
letter follows telephone discussions we have had with you
and Mr. Morodomi since submitting the Request.

As you know, the Request describes the situation of
a client of this firm (the "Company") which occasionally is
hired to compute so-called "special tax" formulas under
contract with a public agency in connection with the
establishment of Mello-Roos Districts. The Request asks the
Commission's informal written assistance to the effect that
the Company's employees do not have an impermissible
conflict of interest under the Political Reform Act when
they perform these services.

It is our understanding, based on telephone
conversations with you and Mr. Morodomi, that there is
tentative agreement with our analysis of the issues we
presented in the Request, although you have recently
identified an additional issue -- the question of whether
there exists an impermissible "nexus," as defined in
Regulation Section 18702.1(d) -- that could present a
conflict of interest. From these telephone conversations,

MORRISON & FOERSTER

John Wallace, Esq.
August 2, 1991
Page Two

we also understood that one type of situation in which the
Company sometimes finds itself raises no conflict of
interest concern. This situation, not described in the
Request, is one where the Company is not involved in
preparing the developer's proposal for formation of the
district, but has its involvement when the public
agency asks the Company to evaluate the submitted proposal.
(The situation described in the Request is one where the
Company is involved at the proposal development stage as
well as after the proposal has been submitted.)

Recently, in the course of our preliminary
discussions on the "nexus" issue, we had the opportunity to
describe the nature of the Company's work in more detail
than set forth in the Request. Based on this description,
you suggested that we provide the Commission with a fuller
written description of the Company's services. We
understand that, depending on the facts, the Company's
employees might not be "consultants" in the performance of
the services in question, in which case they would not be
"public officials" and no conflict of interest issue would
arise.

Although you said you do not want a full legal
"brief" on the issue, we will note here that, in general, a
"public official" includes a "consultant" of a state or
local governmental agency. Gov. Code Section 82048. A
"consultant" includes "any natural person who provides under
contract, information, advice, recommendation or counsel to
a state or local government agency," but does not include a
person who (1) does so "independent of control and direction
of the agency or any agency official, other than normal
contract monitoring," and (2) generally possesses no
"authority with respect to any agency decision." Reg.
Section 18700(a)(2).

Employees of the Company have no official
decision-making authority with regard to the formation of a
district, so the second element of Section 18700(a)(2) is
clearly met. We also believe the employees provide the
Company's services independent of the control and direction
of the public agency.

When the Company is hired by the public agency, it
is for the purpose of preparing a "special tax formula in
connection with a proposed district to be formed by the

MORRISON & FOERSTER

John Wallace, Esq.
August 2, 1991
Page Three

agency. This special tax formula is used in apportioning
special tax rates among assessor's parcels within the
district to pay debt service on bonds issued to finance
public infrastructure. The Company's role is essentially
that of a "number cruncher": it has developed particular
computer programs and expertise in calculating how these
special taxes can be "spread" based on variables provided to
the Company by the agency. The agency and its team of bond
counsel, underwriters and others develop various possible
methods for the financing of the proposed district. As
these various methods are developed, select key input
variables, such as the desired level of tax, the maturity of
each bond issue, and the timing and cost of contemplated
public improvements, may be changed. As these input
variables change, they are fed to the Company, whose
employees perform the necessary calculations to produce new
iterations of a special tax formula. While the Company's
services are obviously important in the over-all effort to
finalize the plan for a proposed district, the Company,
because it alone possesses this technical expertise, works
independently of the agency in performing the special
calculations required for developing the special tax
formula.

     We believe these facts show that the Company's
employees are not acting as "consultants" when they work
with public agencies. However, if the Commission disagrees,
then the recently-raised issue of whether there is a "nexus"
will remain. We have not yet had a full opportunity to
present our analysis on this point, and would appreciate the
opportunity to discuss it with the Commission if this matter
is not resolved on the basis of the "consultant" issue.

     We look forward to hearing from you. Please call
me should you have any questions or comments.

Very truly yours,

Mark R. Burrill

KDD/mab
Enclosures
cc: Kevin D. DeBre, Esq.


[19082(KDD1)]

MORRISON & FOERSTER
LOS ANGELES
SAN FRANCISCO
WALNUT CREEK
PALO ALTO
DENVER

ATTORNEYS AT LAW

19900 MacARTHUR BOULEVARD
IRVINE, CALIFORNIA 92715
TELEPHONE (714) 251-7500
TELEFACSIMILE (714) 251-0900

NEW YORK
WASHINGTON, D.C.
LONDON
HONG KONG
TOKYO

February 22, 1991

VIA CERTIFIED MAIL -- RETURN RECEIPT REQUESTED

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, California 95804-0807

Attention: Mark T. Morodomi, Esq.

Re: Request for Informal Assistance

Gentlemen and Ladies:

This is a request for informal written assistance
from the Fair Political Practices Commission under
Regulation 18329(c).1/ We have been authorized to request
this assistance by a client whose obligations under the
Political Reform Act, Government Code § 81000 et seq. (the
"Act"), are in question.

Our client (the "Company") is a California
corporation which from time to time provides special tax
consulting services under contract to local government
agencies in connection with the establishment of so-called
Mello-Roos Districts under the Mello-Roos Community
Facilities Act of 1982 (Government Code § 53311 et seq.)
The Company's principal work is to prepare the methodology

___________________________
1/ All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6
of the California Code of Regulations.

MORRISON & FOERSTER

Fair Political Practices Commission
February 22, 1991
Page Two

for allocating (or "spreading") the Mello-Roos taxes over
the relevant property.2/

QUESTION

Do the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act
preclude non-owner employees of the Company ("Employees")
from providing tax consulting services to a public agency in
connection with the establishment of a Mello-Roos District
where the Company has performed similar services for the
developer of the real property located within the proposed
Mello-Roos District within the preceding 12 months?

PROPOSED CONCLUSION

The Company's Employees do not have an
impermissible conflict of interest under the Act when they
perform Mello-Roos tax consulting services for a public
agency under the circumstances described above. It is not
reasonably foreseeable, in light of the fact that the tax
consulting services required in establishing a Mello-Roos
District must be complete or substantially complete before
the District is approved or disapproved, that the Company
would receive any material amount of future business from
the developer in connection with the proposed District as a
result of the decision whether to establish the District.

FACTS

From time to time, the Company provides preliminary
Mello-Roos tax consulting services on a fee basis (with fees
exceeding $250) to a land developer as part of the
developer's preparation of a proposal (sometimes in
conjunction with one or more other developers) to the
relevant public agency for the establishment of a Mello-Roos
District. (The relevant agency is the city or county having
jurisdiction over the area of the proposed District.) In
some instances, the public agency will then request the

2/ The Mello-Roos Act provides for the establishment of
special bond financing districts by local government
agencies to finance the construction of community facilities
(for example, fire stations, libraries, schools and parks)
within the area covered by the Mello-Roos District. Id.
§§ 53313, 53313.5. The bonds are repaid by special taxes
levied on the real property located within the Mello-Roos
District. Id. § 53358.

MORRISON & FOERSTER

Fair Political Practices Commission
February 22, 1991
Page Three

Company to contract directly with the public agency to
provide additional consulting services with respect to the
same proposal in order to assist the public agency in
deciding whether to recommend establishing the District.3/
It is possible that employees of the Company who worked on
the project for the developer may thereafter work on the
project for the public agency. However, the Company does
not work for the developer and the public agency with
respect to the same project simultaneously.

The Company is privately held, and no Employee has
a 10 percent or greater ownership interest in the the
Company.

ANALYSIS

Under § 87100 of the Act, no public official may
participate in making a governmental decision in which the
official has a financial interest.

A.     Public Official.

You may assume for purposes of this letter that
Employees of the Company are "consultants" as defined in
Regulation 18700(a)(2) when the Company is providing Mello-
Roos tax consulting services under contract to a public
agency. A consultant is considered to be a public official
subject to the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act.

In contrast, since the Company is not a natural
person, the Company is not a consultant or a public
official. Regulation 18700(a).

B.     Participation in a Governmental Decision.

You may also assume for purposes of this letter
that the tax consulting services rendered by the Employees
to a public agency in connection with the establishment of a
Mello-Roos District constitute participation in the making
of a governmental decision under Regulation 18700(c)(2) (the
giving of advice without significant intervening review by
the public agency).

_________________
3/ After a public agency recommends the establishment of a
Mello-Roos District, two-thirds of the relevant electors
must vote to finally approve the District before it may be
established. See Government Code § 53326.

MORRISON & FOERSTER

Fair Political Practices Commission
February 22, 1991
Page Four

C.      Foreseeable Material Financial Effect.

     The remaining issue thus is whether, under the Act
and applicable regulations, the Employees have a "financial
interest" in the Mello-Roos decision in light of the fact
that the Company previously performed services for one or
more relevant land developers in connection with the
project.

          (i)   Sources of Income to the Employees.

     Section 87103(c) of the Act provides, in
pertinent part, that a public official has a financial
interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that
the decision will have a material financial effect
(distinguishable from its effect on the public generally) on
any source of income of $250 or more to the official within
the 12 months prior to the time the decision is made.  Thus,
the first question is who or what constitutes a source of
income of $250 or more in the preceding 12 months to the
Employees when they are providing Mello-Roos tax consulting
services as described above?

     The regular wages paid to the Employees make
the Company such a source of income to Employees.  In
contrast, since none of the Employees has a 10 percent or
greater ownership interest in the Company, sources of income
to the Company (for example, the developer) are not sources
of income to the Employees.  See Act § 82030(a); FPPC letter
to Diane Fishburn, No. A-90-386 (July 9, 1990) ("Fishburn
Letter") at note 4.

          (ii)  Material Financial Effect on the Company.

     Thus, the only issue remaining with respect to
the Employees is whether a public agency's decision
regarding a proposed Mello-Roos District will have a
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on the
Company.

     The applicable rules for determining the
materiality of the effect of a governmental decision on the
Company appear in Regulation 18702.2(g) (materiality rules
for privately held corporations).  See Act § 82030(a);
Fishburn Letter 7-8.  The test is entirely forward-
looking.  Under the regulation, the effect of a governmental
decision is material as to a business entity if the decision
will result in:

MORRISON & FOERSTER

Fair Political Practices Commission
February 22, 1991
Page Five

(a) an increase or decrease in the
entity's gross revenues for a fiscal
year of $10,000 or more;

(b) the entity's incurring or avoiding
additional expenses or reducing or
eliminating existing expenses for a
fiscal year of $2,500 or more; or

(c) an increase or decrease in the value
of the entity's assets or liabilities
of $10,000 or more.

The question thus is whether it is reasonably
foreseeable that a Mello-Roos decision will have any of the
above effects on the Company.

With respect to the second and third criteria
listed above, you may assume that a public agency's
Mello-Roos decision has no reasonably foreseeable effect on
the Company's expenses or asset or liability values.

With respect to the first criterion (effect on
future income), the FPPC staff considered a similar question
in the Fishburn Letter, where a private civil engineering
consulting company, Cella Barr Associates ("CBA"), had an
ongoing relationship with a land developer. CBA proposed to
prepare the "specific plan" for an undeveloped area under
contract to the relevant city. The area included a
significant amount of land owned by CBA's long-time client,
the developer. The FPPC staff found that under the
circumstances it was "likely" that the developer would
retain CBA for future services with respect to the property
in the planning area if the specific plan (which CBA was to
prepare) approved the property for development. Fishburn
Letter at 6. The FPPC thus concluded that the CBA staff
would have an impermissible conflict of interest if, as
seemed likely, the additional development-related work CBA
would receive from the developer in the planning area would
result in an increase or decrease in gross revenues of
$10,000 or more to CBA in a fiscal year. Id. at 8.

The Company's situation is entirely different,
due to the different nature of Mello-Roos work, and a
different conclusion thus is called for. Unlike CBA's
situation, there is no future prospect of the Company's
receiving additional tax consulting work from the developer
with respect to a given project after the public agency has

MORRISON & FOERSTER

Fair Political Practices Commission
February 22, 1991
Page Six

retained the Company on the project. This is because the
tax consulting work on a Mello-Roos District is
substantially completed by the time the public agency
recommends the approval or disapproval of the District. The
tax spread methodology must be completed in advance because
it is part of the Mello-Roos proposal which the agency
approves or disapproves. As a result, neither the approval
nor disapproval of a Mello-Roos District can foreseeably
result in the relevant developer's sending additional
business to the Company in connection with that District.4/

     Our client's actual experience bears this analysis
out. Over the course of several years and several dozen
projects, there has been no case in which the relevant
developer has retained the Company for significant
additional work on a particular proposed Mello-Roos District
after the time the public agency has retained the Company
directly to work on that proposed District. (In a few
cases, developers have asked the Company to prepare a short
consumer disclosure about the District after it is approved.
This work has never resulted in more than $2,500 of
additional revenue to the Company.) Likewise (again unlike
CBA's situation in the Fishburn Letter), the Company does
not perform general civil engineering, planning or other
services that could result in additional income if a
Mello-Roos District is approved.

     In light of the foregoing, under the tests
established by Regulation 18702.2(g), no Employee of the
Company should be deemed to have a financial interest in the
decision by a public agency whether to establish a
Mello-Roos District, even where the Company has provided
preliminary Mello-Roos tax consulting services within the
preceding 12 months to the relevant developer. As a result,
the conflict of interest provisions of the Act should not

_____________________

4/ In our telephone conversation on January 9, 1991, you
asked whether the developer might retain the Company for
future work on other Mello-Roos Districts. Although such a
possibility cannot entirely be excluded, it is not
reasonably foreseeable (as required by the Act to create a
conflict of interest) that such future business would be
forthcoming or that any such future business would be the
result of the decision on a prior Mello-Roos District. As
noted in the Fishburn Letter, obtaining future work from a
developer on an unrelated project is an uncertain
expectation. Id. at note 5.

MORRISON & FOERSTER

Fair Political Practices Commission
February 22, 1991
Page Seven

preclude an Employee from acting as a consultant to the
relevant public agency in connection with the Mello-Roos
decision process under such circumstances.

* * *

Please do not hesitate to telephone Mark R. Burrill
in our Los Angeles office should you have any questions or
comments regarding the foregoing. Mark's direct dial number
is (213) 621-9577.

Very truly yours,

Robert K. Olsen

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Do the conf f ict-of- interest provisions of the Act
preclude non-owner employees of the Company (,,Employees,,)
from providing tax consulting services to a public agency in
connection with the establishment of a Melfo-Roos District
where the Conpany has performed similar services for the
developer of the real property located within the proposed
Mello-Roos District within the preceding 12 nonths?
PROPOSED CONCLUS ION
The Companyts Employees do not have an
imperrnissibl-e conflict of interest under the Act when they
perform Me1fo-Roos tax consulting services for a public
agency under the circumstances described above. It is not
reasonably foreseeable, in light of the fact that the tax
consulting services required in establishing a MeLlo-Roos
District must be complete or substantiatly cornplete before
the District is approved or disapproved, that the Company
would receive any material anount of future business from
the developer in connection with the proposed District as a
result of the decision whether to establish the District.
embedding.first_500_words: MORRISON & FOERSTER ATTORNEYS AT LAW SAN FRANCISCO ORANGE COUNTY WALNUT CREEK PALO ALTO DENVER 333 SOUTH GRAND AVENUE LOS ANGELES, CA 90071-3168 TELEPHONE (213) 626-3800 TELEFACSIMILE (213) 626-6955 TELEX 261823 MOFO UR NEW YORK WASHINGTON, D.C. LONDON HONG KONG TOKYO DIRECT DIAL NUMBER August 2, 1991 (213) 621-9577 VIA FEDERAL EXPRESS Fair Political Practices Commission 428 J Street, Suite 800 Sacramento, California 95804-0807 Attention: John Wallace, Esq. Re: Letter No. 91-118 Dear Mr. Wallace: This letter is in reference to Letter No. 91-118 (the "Request"), a request for informal assistance, dated February 22, 1991, submitted by Robert K. Olsen of our firm to Mark T. Morodomi, an attorney with the Commission. This letter follows telephone discussions we have had with you and Mr. Morodomi since submitting the Request. As you know, the Request describes the situation of a client of this firm (the "Company") which occasionally is hired to compute so-called "special tax" formulas under contract with a public agency in connection with the establishment of Mello-Roos Districts. The Request asks the Commission's informal written assistance to the effect that the Company's employees do not have an impermissible conflict of interest under the Political Reform Act when they perform these services. It is our understanding, based on telephone conversations with you and Mr. Morodomi, that there is tentative agreement with our analysis of the issues we presented in the Request, although you have recently identified an additional issue -- the question of whether there exists an impermissible "nexus," as defined in Regulation Section 18702.1(d) -- that could present a conflict of interest. From these telephone conversations, MORRISON & FOERSTER John Wallace, Esq. August 2, 1991 Page Two we also understood that one type of situation in which the Company sometimes finds itself raises no conflict of interest concern. This situation, not described in the Request, is one where the Company is not involved in preparing the developer's proposal for formation of the district, but has its involvement when the public agency asks the Company to evaluate the submitted proposal. (The situation described in the Request is one where the Company is involved at the proposal development stage as well as after the proposal has been submitted.) Recently, in the course of our preliminary discussions on the "nexus" issue, we had the opportunity to describe the nature of the Company's work in more detail than set forth in the Request. Based on this description, you suggested that we provide the Commission with a fuller written description of the Company's services. We understand that, depending on the facts, the Company's employees might not be "consultants" in the performance of the services in question, in which case they would not be "public officials" and no conflict of interest issue would arise. Although you said you do not want a full legal "brief" on the issue, we will note here that, in general, a "public official" includes a "consultant" of a state or local governmental agency. Gov. Code Section 82048. A "consultant" includes
